title: Public Works Committee
overview: The Public Works Committee approved several infrastructure projects and discussed ongoing capital improvements. Key decisions included awarding a $126,900 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel, approving a construction agreement with Rothschild for the Military Road project ($94,860 Weston share), and directing staff to create enforcement language for sanitary sewer discharge violations with fines up to $100/day. The committee also approved construction service proposals for the Alderson/Jelinek roundabout (MSA) and Bladel Avenue (Clark Dietz) projects.

agenda.item: Call to order
Pledge of Allegiance
Roll call
Public comment
Approval of April 13, 2026 meeting minutes
Acknowledge April 2026 water and sewer permits
CIP update
Street operation update
Utility operating update
Review 2025 Water Utility PSC annual report
Resolution 2026-014 transportation funding
Recycled asphalt base course production
Sanitary sewer discharge violation discussion
Water and sewer connection agreement for Radkey Avenue duplex
Military Road construction agreement
Construction service proposals for Alderson/Jelinek and Bladel projects

discussions.body: Michael reported the village received a Local Road Improvement Program (LRIP) grant for Birch Street reconstruction between Schofield Avenue and Community Center Drive, originally a 2029 project that will likely move to 2028. The grant covers over 50% of the estimated $1.5 million project cost. Michael noted the village has received over $9 million in grant funding over the past 4-5 years. Discussion also covered ongoing repairs to Weston Avenue by Integrity Grading and Excavating for road settling over sewer trenches, and HOS Construction's upcoming repairs on Schofield Avenue including pavement marking, manhole repairs, and concrete work.

Staff reported on spring activities including road limit removal on April 29th, mailbox and sign repairs, dewatering operations near Weston Avenue tributary for approximately one week, spring curb line sweeping, and hot mix patching. Storm damage from East Nick required tree removal. Culvert failures on Jones Street required installation of a third 18-inch culvert. Equipment maintenance included removing plow equipment from trucks and servicing mowing equipment. Mac tandem axle chassis have been ordered with upfitter items for the first truck.

Josh reported on water service repair at Caribou Coffee, ongoing well maintenance, and meter card maintenance. Sanitary side showed significant clear water infiltration issues with lift station pumps working overtime. April flow data showed 37 million gallons pumped into the system but 53-54 million gallons treated, indicating approximately 15 million gallons of clear water infiltration. Discussion focused on residents connecting sump pumps to sanitary sewers. The INI (infiltration and inflow) study with Inpipe Technologies will help identify problem areas. Well two rehab at Carey started that day.

Michael presented the annual Public Service Commission report showing net operating income of $482,911 for 2025, slightly lower than 2024's $490,000. Water loss is at 6%. Utility value increased to nearly $34 million, a 6.7% increase due to infrastructure placed in service including wells 7 and 8, and water main from the Weston Avenue project. The utility serves approximately 6,100 customers total: 5,250 in Weston, 830 in Rothschild, and 5 in Schofield or Rib Mountain Metro area. Motion to acknowledge passed unanimously.

Michael explained this League of Wisconsin Municipalities sample resolution supports long-term funding solutions for the state transportation fund. He noted that while grant funding helps one-time costs, recurring annual payments for street maintenance and snow plowing are inadequately funded, requiring subsidy from general tax dollars. Wisconsin remains relatively affordable compared to neighboring states for automobile ownership costs. Motion to recommend village board adoption of Resolution 2026-014 passed unanimously.

Michael presented quotes for crushing recycled concrete and asphalt at the Ryan Street site. King Gravel was low bidder at $4.23/ton for an estimated 30,000 tons totaling $126,900. Michael demonstrated the cost savings: village cost is $17.11/cubic yard versus $28/cubic yard that City of Wausau pays, saving approximately $130,000 across three 2026 projects. Motion to award to King Gravel passed unanimously.

Staff sought guidance on enforcing violations for clear water discharge into sanitary sewers. Current code allows fines of $100-$1,000 per day per violation. Committee discussed distinguishing between unknowing violations (usually corrected with notice) and willful violations. Trustee Zogami suggested waiting for the Inpipe Technologies study results, then sending violation letters with 30-60 day correction periods. Committee agreed on $100/day starting fine. Motion to direct staff to create enforcement language with fines not to exceed $100/day passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Water and Sewer Connection Agreement - Radkey Avenue
discussions.body: Michael explained a duplex developer at 2230 Radkey Avenue needs utility connection, but mains currently dead-end at Radkey Avenue. Staff proposed allowing a temporary long lateral connection through the ditch rather than requiring the developer to install mains through Outlot 1. When Radkey Avenue is reconstructed (currently planned for 2029), the village will assess main extension costs to the property owner. Committee requested adding a date requirement. Motion to recommend approval with reconstruction no later than end of 2029 construction season passed unanimously.

The agreement with Village of Rothschild covers construction from Wolfman Street to Helin Street. Weston's share is $94,860 for sanitary sewer ($6,156) and water main relocation ($88,704) necessitated by storm sewer improvements. Rothschild holds the contract with Steam Construction. The water main will have approximately 18 inches of cover with 8 inches of insulation. Motion to recommend village board approval passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Construction Service Proposals - Alderson/Jelinek and Bladel Projects
discussions.body: Michael presented proposals received late Friday. For Alderson/Jelinek roundabout: MSA proposed $37,315 versus Clark Dietz at $48,910. For Bladel Avenue: MSA proposed $70,400 versus Clark Dietz at $73,730. Michael recommended MSA for the roundabout (lower cost, assisted with roundabout design) and Clark Dietz for Bladel (full-time experienced staff versus MSA's summer intern, minimal cost difference). Motion to approve MSA for roundabout and Clark Dietz for Bladel passed unanimously.

actionItems: Award 2026 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel for $126,900 estimated total
Recommend village board adopt Resolution 2026-014 supporting state transportation funding solutions
Direct staff to create enforcement language for sanitary sewer discharge violations with fines not to exceed $100/day
Recommend village board approve Radkey Avenue temporary utility connection agreement with reconstruction deadline of end of 2029
Recommend village board approve Military Road construction agreement with Rothschild for $94,860 Weston share
Approve MSA for Alderson/Jelinek roundabout construction services and Clark Dietz for Bladel Avenue construction services
Schedule Bladel Avenue special assessment meeting for Thursday, May 28, 2026 at 6:00 PM
Norcon to begin Schofield Avenue concrete repairs in eastbound lanes starting Wednesday/Thursday this week
Staff to provide cost analysis of clear water treatment expenses for next meeting
Staff to provide update on Schofield Avenue street lighting

title: Tourism Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Tourism Commission held its first meeting after reorganization, electing new officers and approving a $5,000 grant for the Taste and Glow Balloonfest. The commission learned that a competing hot air balloon event in Marshfield was canceled, potentially increasing attendance at the Weston event.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Roll call
Election of officers - Chair
Election of officers - Vice Chair
Election of officers - Secretary
Public comments
Approval of previous meeting minutes (April 21st)
New business - Grant request for Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Remarks from commission members
Adjournment

discussions.body: Trustee Erling was nominated and elected as Chair unanimously. Cushing was nominated by a commissioner and seconded by Jackson, then elected Vice Chair unanimously. Renee was nominated and elected Secretary unanimously.

discussions.item: Grant Request - Taste and Glow Balloonfest
discussions.body: Brandon Jill of 3308 Green Pastures Lane presented the grant request. He reported last year's attendance was estimated at 11,000-12,000 and announced that the competing Marshfield hot air balloon event was canceled due to permit issues, making Taste and Glow the sole hot air balloon event in the area. A commissioner questioned room usage, noting the application showed 20 fewer rooms than last year. The commission discussed the importance of tracking hotel room data for future decisions. The commission approved a $5,000 grant (same as last year) unanimously, with a request for better hotel room tracking data in the post-event report.

discussions.item: Commission Member Remarks - Alice in Dairyland Event
discussions.body: Renee reported attending the Alice in Dairyland dinner at Red Rock Orchard in Edgar. She noted the candidates were from various Wisconsin universities, and Anastasia won the competition. The candidates toured Marathon County attractions including Mullen's Cheese. This was the first time the event was hosted in Marathon County since the 1970s.

actionItems: Approved $5,000 grant to Taste and Glow Balloonfest for their annual event
Taste and Glow organizers to provide hotel room usage data in their post-event report
Next meeting scheduled for July 20th at 4:00 PM

title: Plan Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Plan Commission approved the site plan for the new Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive, and adopted a new salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection districts. Both items passed unanimously with the commission also receiving public comment requesting fiscal restraint on the police facility landscaping requirements.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call
Open public comment period
Approval of April 13, 2026 meeting minutes
Site plan review - Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive
Creation of salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
April 2026 staff approved certified survey map and site plan
April 2026 building permit
Planning and development department monthly project update report
Commissioner remarks
Future agenda items and staff discussion
Adjournment

discussions.item: Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court Facility Site Plan
discussions.body: Staff presented the site plan for the new police department and municipal court at 3600 Community Center Drive, which meets all B2 zoning district requirements. Police Chief Jeremy explained challenges with a gas pipeline under the parking lot that required rotating the building orientation. The building is designed for 50-60 officer capacity (current staffing is 40 officers and 4 clerical staff) with expansion potential to the north. Discussion addressed an alternate storage shed placement for future expansion flexibility. Motion to recommend approval to the Board of Trustees with staff conditions 2-5 passed unanimously.

Staff proposed a program requiring two annual inspections per facility in wellhead protection districts - one warm weather inspection focusing on structural integrity and drainage, and one winter inspection focusing on operations and containment. A standardized inspection worksheet similar to DOT standards was developed. Staff noted one existing facility near Sternberg that predates the wellhead protection ordinance and has never gone through the conditional use process. Commission agreed staff should reach out to bring them into compliance. Motion to approve the program with reporting back to both Plan Commission and Public Works passed unanimously.
Motion to approve the April 13, 2026 meeting minutes as presented passed unanimously with no discussion.

Staff announced the first joint Plan Commission/Village Board steering committee meeting for the comprehensive plan is scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 5:00 p.m. Discussion occurred about potential residential TIF districts for workforce housing, with staff noting Ehlers has prepared a presentation on qualifications that could be shared with commissioners or incorporated into comprehensive plan discussions.

publicComment: Jim Pensel of 5002 Arrow Street, Weston, spoke via online connection requesting the planning commission exercise fiscal restraint on landscaping requirements for the new police department, citing previous relaxations made for the Greenheck Turner building. He also asked that the salt shed inspection program include annual reporting on site visits and findings.
actionItems: Site plan for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive recommended for Board of Trustees approval
Salt storage shed inspection program adopted for wellhead protection district implementation
Staff to report inspection findings back to Plan Commission and Public Works committee annually
Staff to reach out to existing salt storage facility near Sternberg that lacks conditional use permit
Joint Plan Commission/Village Board steering committee meeting for comprehensive plan scheduled for June 1st at 5:00 p.m.
Staff to send email and calendar event for June 1st steering committee meeting
Electric vehicle charging station regulations added as future agenda item for fall
Next regular meeting scheduled for Monday, June 8th, 2026 at 6:00 p.m.

title: Health & Human Services Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Health & Human Services Committee held an organizational meeting for new board members, featuring educational presentations from the Health Department, Veteran Services, and Aging Disability Resource Center. The committee approved April 1st meeting minutes unanimously and received detailed overviews of department missions, budgets, and current initiatives including homelessness response efforts and veteran suicide prevention programs.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call (Supervisors Kremes and Vice Chair Rosenberg attending remotely)
Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes
Operational functions required by statute - no items for this meeting
Review of standing committee ordinances, missions, duties, and responsibilities
Health Department overview presentation by Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka
Social Services Department overview by Administrator Leonard
Veteran Service Department presentation by VSO Erin Galindo
Aging Disability Resource Center presentation by Executive Director Mike Ray
Next meeting date and adjournment

discussions.body: Supervisor Cavelli made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Vice Chair Rosenberg. The motion passed unanimously with all members voting aye.

Administrator Lance Leonard explained the committee structure under ordinance section 2.04 subsection 5, including jurisdiction over Health Department, Social Services, Veteran Services, North Central Health Care, and ADRC. He outlined statutory requirements and relationships with boards including the Social Services Board, Veteran Service Commission, Transportation Coordinating Committee, and Board of Health. Chair Boots noted the upcoming strategic plan development expected to be completed by December.

Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka presented on the department's 37.6 FTE staff across four divisions: environmental health, nursing (communicable disease and family health), community health improvement, and administration. She highlighted 1,200+ licenses overseen, the Nurse Family Partnership program with first graduates at age 2, new homeless system response position, and public health vending machines offering free resources including tick kits. Administrator Leonard noted roughly half of the $5 million budget is tax levy with environmental health being largely self-supporting through fees.

Administrator Leonard presented on behalf of absent Director Christa, describing the department's nearly 140 FTE staff handling child welfare, youth justice, children's long-term support, economic support, and child support. He highlighted the successful Family Keys pilot program addressing housing instability, noting the program was substantively improved based on county board feedback. The department's $22 million budget includes about $7 million in tax levy.

VSO Erin Galindo reported on the small but mighty 3.0 FTE department that had 8,149 interactions in 2025, up from 5,900. He emphasized the PACT Act's impact on presumptive conditions, noting $44 million annually flows to Marathon County veterans with $2.6 million in retroactive payments in 2025. Key initiatives include surviving spouse benefits outreach, veteran housing stability partnership with Center for Veterans Issues, and suicide prevention efforts following 7 veteran suicides among 30 total county suicides in 2025. Supervisor Kremes asked about regional coordination; Galindo explained state statute requires each county to have a VSO and that regionalizing was previously rejected.

Executive Director Mike Ray described the four-county integrated organization serving Marathon, Lincoln, Langlade, and Wood counties with about 60 FTEs and 385-400 volunteers. He explained services including information assistance, caregiver support, dementia care, falls prevention, and Meals on Wheels. Ray noted significant financial challenges with nutrition program costs up 40-45% in four years while federal funding remained flat, forcing meal and staffing reductions. The organization received its first levy increase in 2024/2025 after remaining flat since formation in 2011. Marathon County's 2026 contribution is $413,135.

actionItems: April 1st meeting minutes approved
North Central Healthcare to be scheduled for separate full presentation before the committee
ADRC Executive Director Mike Ray to present annual report with 2025 data to full board
Committee members to email Chair Boots and Administrator Leonard with suggested future agenda items
Strategic plan development to be discussed at upcoming meetings with completion target of December
Next meeting scheduled for Tuesday, June 3rd at 3:00 PM (corrected from initially stated May 29th)

title: Board of Trustees
overview: The Village of Weston Board of Trustees approved increased park and recreation impact fees, adopted multiple ordinances including mobile food vendor licensing and e-bike regulations, and discussed ongoing challenges with the farmers market's new location at Kennedy Park. The board also adjourned the Board of Review to June 15, 2026 and approved various license renewals and committee appointments.

agenda.item: Board of Review called to order and adjourned to June 15, 2026
Board of Trustees meeting called to order, Pledge of Allegiance, roll call
Public comments - Jim Pitel on engineering consultants and committee appointments
Public hearing on park and recreation impact fee amendments
Approval of meeting minutes from April 13 and April 21, 2026
Acknowledgment of committee reports and chair updates
Work product transmittals including building permits and budget status
Consent agenda - vouchers and license renewals
Ordinances - mobile food vendor, nuisances, e-bikes, traffic
Discussion on farmers market location at Kennedy Park

discussions.item: Public hearing on park and recreation impact fees
discussions.body: Staff presented proposed amendments to increase park fees from $300/$250 to $761 for single family, $563 for duplex, and $495 for multifamily units, based on the 2022 public facilities needs assessment. Jim Pitel commented that the village lacks real data on park maintenance costs. Trustee Motti supported the fee as justified given population growth. Ordinance 26-010 was approved unanimously, and Resolution 2026-015 was also approved unanimously.

Chief Finkey explained that this ordinance allows reciprocal fire inspections between Safer and multiple municipalities including Marshfield, Wisconsin Rapids, Stevens Point, and Cloverleaf. A food truck inspected by one participating department receives a sticker recognized by all others, reducing redundant inspections. Ordinance 26-008 was approved unanimously.

The board debated amendments to the remote attendance policy. After discussion about removing specific reasons for remote attendance to make the policy more broad, Trustee Beck moved to amend by striking sentences requiring health/safety justification. The amendment passed with Trustees Herling and Motti voting no. The original motion as amended then passed with the same two no votes.

President appointed Stephanie Daniels and Goldens Mua to the CLIPS committee. Motion by Herling, second by Lopesell, approved unanimously. Parks, two openings on HR/Finance, and one opening on CLIPS remain.

discussions.item: Secondhand article dealer license for Scoffield Coin and Hobby LLC
discussions.body: Chief Hunt and Captain Aldrich explained the license is required under state statute for businesses buying/selling precious metals to prevent stolen merchandise sales. Items must be held 30 days before sale. Motion was amended to require satisfactory background check and certificate of occupancy since the building is still under construction. Approved unanimously as amended.

discussions.item: Farmers market location at Kennedy Park
discussions.body: Discussion-only item addressing concerns about the market's new location after losing the Camp Phillips site. Trustee Beck raised safety concerns about parking on grass along Alderson. Renee provided background that the market grew from 25 to 53 vendor spots over the years. The board discussed that Mont Miller Park would be too remote. No action was taken; this was discussion only. Renee clarified that the market operates at a profit through vendor fees, grants, and token reimbursements.

publicComment: Jim Pitel of 52nd Street spoke during public comment, criticizing the village for hiring outside engineering consultants at an estimated $108,000 when an engineering technician position remains unfilled. He questioned why the administrator hasn't filled the position and criticized the appointment of Lisa Beck to the Safer board before official board action. He also complained about lack of detailed information on Kennedy Park in the meeting packet. Pitel also commented during the park impact fee public hearing, arguing the village lacks concrete data on park maintenance costs to justify the fee increases.
actionItems: Board of Review adjourned to June 15, 2026
Park and recreation impact fees increased effective upon ordinance and resolution adoption
Mobile food vendor license ordinance adopted enabling reciprocal inspections
E-bike and nuisance ordinance amendments adopted
Remote meeting attendance policy amended to remove specific justification requirements
Stephanie Daniels and Goldens Mua appointed to CLIPS committee
Secondhand dealer license approved for Scoffield Coin and Hobby LLC pending occupancy and background check
Resolution 2026-014 adopted supporting sustainable state transportation funding
Comprehensive plan steering committee first meeting scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 6:00 PM
Staff to conduct speed study on Weston Avenue and bring results back to board
Intergovernmental service agreement to be drafted for Ringle building inspection assistance

title: Regular Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Board of Supervisors held an adjourned organizational meeting where they approved committee appointments, elected Supervisors Hapa and Arstead to the Executive Committee, and unanimously passed a resolution reducing landfill tipping fees for disaster-related debris following severe weather that impacted the Ringle area. The board suspended its rules to consider the emergency tipping fee resolution directly from the Solid Waste Management Board.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance and moment of reflection
Reading of notice and roll call
Committee appointments discussion and remarks from Chair
Election of at-large Executive Committee members
Consent agenda items C10 through C14A
Resolution D15: Out of home placement reserve increase
Resolution D16: Amend social services budget
Resolution E17: Modification to landfill tipping fees
Vote on landfill tipping fee resolution
Announcements and adjournment

discussions.body: Chair Gibbs outlined goals for committee appointments including increasing diversity of perspectives, ensuring supervisor development opportunities, and noting that over 34% of the board is in its first or second term. Vice Chair Boots assisted in developing appointments. The motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Crims, was carried but not unanimous. Supervisor Arstead noted the system marked him as absent but he was present and an aye vote.

discussions.item: Election of At-Large Executive Committee Members
discussions.body: Three supervisors were nominated: Supervisor Hapa (nominated by Supervisor Mask), Supervisor Leur (nominated by Supervisor Rosenberg), and Supervisor Arstead (nominated by Vice Chair Boots). Supervisor Rosenberg explained her nomination of Supervisor Leur, citing her advocacy for the county's youngest children and oldest elders. Paper ballots were distributed and supervisors selected two names. Supervisors Hapa and Arstead were elected to the Executive Committee.
Items C10 through C14A were approved on motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Resolution D15: Out of Home Placement Reserve Increase
discussions.body: Resolution 24-26 was moved by Supervisor Morash and seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The motion carried but was not unanimous.

discussions.item: Resolution D16: Amend Social Services Budget
discussions.body: Resolution 25-26 was moved by Supervisor P and seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The motion carried but was not unanimous.

Administrator Leonard and Director Hagen Busher presented a proposal to reduce tipping fees for disaster-related debris following severe weather that damaged approximately 44 homes in Ringle. The state waived its $13.10 fee, Ringle waived its $2.33 fee, and the proposal reduced the county's rate to $36.94 per ton (covering operational costs). Two residents, Jessica Toasty and Matt Haybeck, were granted special dispensation to speak, requesting the county waive fees entirely for six months citing financial hardships and delayed insurance adjusters. Multiple supervisors asked clarifying questions about fee structures, eligibility, and future policy development. The board first voted unanimously to suspend rules to consider the resolution directly from the Solid Waste Management Board, then passed Resolution 26-26 unanimously on motion by Supervisor Arstead, seconded by Supervisor Morash.

publicComment: Two residents were granted special dispensation to address the board during the tipping fee discussion. Jessica Toasty spoke about the community being self-reliant in cleanup, noting one business spent $40,000 in hauling and cleanup expenses, and requested the county waive fees for six months. Matt Haybeck spoke on behalf of approximately 44 affected homeowners, requesting fee waivers for underinsured residents and noting many insurance adjusters have not yet arrived.
actionItems: Committee appointments approved as presented by Chair Gibbs and Vice Chair Boots
Supervisors Hapa and Arstead elected to Executive Committee at-large positions
Resolution 26-26 approved reducing landfill tipping fees to $36.94 per ton for certified disaster-related debris through June 17, 2025
Administrator Leonard to continue discussions with community foundation about reimbursement mechanism for unreimbursed out-of-pocket debris disposal costs
Solid waste management board to develop clear policy within six months for handling similar disaster situations in the future
Board to revisit tipping fee reduction at May meeting if extension beyond state declaration period is needed

title: Environmental Resources Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Environmental Resources Committee approved two rezoning petitions and voted to forward a modified fee waiver resolution to the county board that will only waive reconnect fees for septic systems for properties damaged in the April 17, 2026 tornado. The committee also received department overviews from Solid Waste, Parks Recreation and Forestry, and Conservation Planning and Zoning.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Public comment
Approval of March 31st, 2026 committee minutes
Public hearing: Tim Veland rezoning petition (urban residential/general agriculture to rural residential) - Town of Green Valley
Public hearing: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (conservancy recreation to general agriculture) - Town of McMillan
Consideration of fee waivers for properties impacted by April 17, 2026 severe weather
Department overview: Solid Waste
Department overview: Parks, Recreation, and Forestry
Department overview: Conservation, Planning, and Zoning

discussions.body: Supervisor Sefeld made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: Tim Veland rezoning petition - Town of Green Valley
discussions.body: Land Use Specialist Garrett Pogle presented the staff report for rezoning from urban residential and general agriculture to rural residential for a proposed land division. The Town of Green Valley recommended approval on March 10, 2026. No public comment was offered. Supervisor Drebeck moved to approve based on findings of fact and conclusions of law, seconded by Supervisor Kurt. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition - Town of McMillan
discussions.body: Garrett Pogle presented the petition to rezone from conservancy recreation to general agriculture. Supervisor Leur asked about the conservancy nature, and Pogle explained the property contains a home and horse riding stable, and rezoning would make the home a conforming use rather than non-conforming. He confirmed no natural area would be lost. The Town of McMillan recommended approval on April 20, 2026. No public comment was offered. Supervisor Sefeld moved to approve, seconded by Supervisor Drew. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Fee waivers for April 17, 2026 severe weather damage
discussions.body: Director Lori Mskimmons presented staff recommendations to waive county zoning and septic fees for properties damaged by the tornado affecting the towns of Ringle, Eastston, and villages of Cronenwetter and Weston. Supervisor Drew questioned where to draw the line for future events. Supervisor Leur asked about budget impacts. Supervisor Drebeck raised concerns about fairness to those not in declared disaster areas and questioned consistency with recent fee increases. After debate, Supervisor Drebeck proposed an amendment to limit fee waivers to only reconnect fees for septic systems ($160), excluding other pouts and zoning fees. The amendment was seconded by Supervisor Kurt and passed. The amended resolution was then approved to forward to the county board, though not unanimously.

Director David Hagenbusher presented an overview of the Solid Waste Department, highlighting the renewable natural gas partnership with Vidi Energy launched in September 2025, significant capital expenses for landfill expansion and closure, and challenges with leachate treatment due to PFAS regulations requiring on-site treatment by 2032. He noted the department operates as an enterprise fund with 11 FTEs processing about 1,000 tons daily.

discussions.item: Department overview: Parks, Recreation, and Forestry
discussions.body: County Forest Administrator Tom Lovelin presented on behalf of Director Jamie, covering the joint city-county department structure with 44 full-time and 120 seasonal employees. He highlighted Nine Mile County Forest generating $3.25 million in tourism spending and noted committee structure changes creating a five-member county forestry parks and recreation committee. Supervisor Sefeld acknowledged Lovelin's retirement effective June 1st after nearly 30 years of service.

publicComment: No public comment was offered. No one signed up for public comment at the beginning of the meeting, and no members of the public spoke during the public hearing portions for either rezoning petition.
actionItems: Forward amended resolution to county board waiving only reconnect fees ($160) for septic systems for properties damaged by April 17, 2026 severe weather
Forward James Offer/David Tract rezoning approval to Marathon County Board of Supervisors
Forward Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning approval to Marathon County Board of Supervisors
Town of Green Valley and Town of McMillan recommended to update comprehensive plans to reflect zoning changes if applicable
CPZ to track costs and requests related to tornado fee waivers for future policy development
Conservation services educational presentation to be scheduled for future summer meeting

title: Parks Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Parks Committee deferred discussion on allowing dogs at the aquatic center due to the chair's absence, approved installation of new kayak launch signs along the Plover River, and received updates on Kennedy Park fundraising and trail maintenance. Public commenters expressed opposition to the dog day concept and raised questions about Kennedy Park financial transparency.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Roll call
Approval of minutes from April 27, 2026
Public comments
Kennedy Park update
Yellow Banks disc golf course update
Dog Day at the aquatic center
Graffiti on the Oaklair River Trail
Yellow Banks kayak launch signs
Topics for future meetings and staff remarks

discussions.body: Minutes from the April 27, 2026 meeting were approved. A motion was made and seconded, with the motion carrying unanimously with no opposition.

Staff presented a timeline document tracing Kennedy Park development from the comprehensive outdoor recreation plan approval in November 2023 through the May 9th ribbon cutting. Committee member Scott asked clarifying questions about village capital funds and fundraising timelines. Staff explained the Friends of Kennedy Park are targeting $117,000 for field lights and currently have approximately $84-85,000 at the community foundation. Staff noted the Friends group plans a public check presentation at their June tournament. A committee member emphasized the Friends group deserves 'grace' as volunteers and that master plans are guidance documents subject to change.

discussions.item: Yellow Banks disc golf course update
discussions.body: Staff presented photos and maps showing tree removal locations along the river and wood mat installation over gas pipelines for equipment access. Dan provided information on the mat installation process, noting the mats protect TC Energy (formerly ANR/ATC) gas pipelines. The village now has extra mats that may be used on another trail project.

discussions.item: Dog Day at the aquatic center
discussions.body: The committee deferred this item to the next meeting because Katrina, who requested the agenda item, was absent due to an unforeseen issue. A resident who spoke during public comment (Andy from 6504 [street name inaudible]) stated his objection would be noted and indicated he may return next month.

discussions.item: Graffiti on the Oaklair River Trail
discussions.body: Staff explained challenges removing graffiti from concrete and benches on the trail, noting their best tool (a sandblaster through pressure washer) cannot access the trail due to bridge weight limits and terrain. Committee discussed options including anti-vandalism coatings, seal coats, and potential volunteer workdays. Staff will investigate cleaning solutions and potentially address items during off-season by rotating benches out for cleaning.

Dan Kramer from 2625 Northwestern Avenue presented version seven of proposed kayak launch signs for Plover River landings. Signs will be installed at Highway J, Ross Avenue, and Yellow Banks locations in Weston, plus locations in Schofield. The signs include maps, QR codes for river gauge information, and location-specific historical content. Marathon County Parks Foundation provided funding through state grants. The committee approved a motion to install the signs, which passed unanimously.

Staff raised a request received via email about installing food allergy awareness signage at playgrounds, noting one in 13 people have food allergies. Committee agreed to bring the topic back for further discussion. Staff also announced the Mock Miller park master plan process will begin with a kickoff meeting with Rettler planned for next Tuesday.

publicComment: Two people provided public comment. Andy (6504 [street inaudible]) spoke in person opposing dogs at the aquatic center, stating 'There should not be animals allowed into a public pool. Period. I like dogs, but it's not a place for them. We have rivers. We have lakes. That's where they belong.' Jim Pensan (5002 [street] Weston), speaking online under the name 'Vern M,' criticized the 4 p.m. meeting time as discouraging public participation and questioned Kennedy Park finances, calling the $8,700 expenditure figure 'too coincidental' and asking about the $10,000 grant and glow event funds, stating 'I think the numbers presented are extremely convoluted and misleading.'
actionItems: Dog Day at the aquatic center discussion deferred to June meeting
Motion approved to install Plover River kayak launch signs at village locations
Staff to investigate graffiti removal options and potential anti-vandalism coatings for trail amenities
Food allergy awareness playground signage to be brought back for future discussion
Mock Miller park master plan kickoff meeting with Rettler scheduled for next Tuesday
Next meeting scheduled for June 22, 2026

title: Finance and Human Resources Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Finance and Human Resources Committee approved previous meeting minutes and acknowledged budget reports for April. The meeting featured an extensive educational presentation from Municipal Clerk Pam on the clerk's office operations, including elections, licensing, tax collection, and the newsletter budget. Committee member Evan announced he is stepping down as this was his last meeting.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Roll call
Public comments
Approval of minutes from previous Finance and HR meeting
April budget status report
TIFF one and TIFF two detail reports for April
Legal details report for April
Educational presentation - Municipal Clerk's budget and office overview
Staff remarks and farmers market update
Committee member announcements
Adjournment

discussions.item: Approval of minutes from previous Finance and HR meeting
discussions.body: Motion to approve made by Ernling, seconded by Daniels. No discussion. Motion carried unanimously with voice vote.

discussions.item: April budget status report and TIFF reports
discussions.body: Jessica presented the April budget status report and TIFF one and TIFF two detail reports. A committee member asked about the timeline for TIFF closure, and Jessica explained they are anticipating October-November for bond payoff. The CDA will need to pass a resolution to call the bonds, which are due October 1st. Jessica confirmed the numbers still look good and they will be able to pay off the debt. Motion to acknowledge items 5, 6, and 7 made by Daniels, seconded by Soaper. Motion carried unanimously.

Clerk Pam presented an extensive overview of the clerk's office operations. The office currently has two full-time employees (Pam and Sarah) and one part-time employee (Leah), down from four full-time staff previously. Pam explained that 84% of tax collection ($22 million) is handled at the clerk's office counter between mid-December and January 31st. Discussion covered election costs, noting postage increased from $6,000 to $15,000 due to fall elections and absentee ballots. The quarterly newsletter costs approximately $30,000 annually ($9,000 postage, $20,000 printing) for 5,089 postal patrons. Committee discussed potentially reducing newsletter costs, but staff noted previous attempts to eliminate mailing resulted in resident complaints requesting its return.

Pam explained that pet licensing fees increased from $10 to $20 because Marathon County raised fees charged to municipalities to help cover their $100,000 annual payment to the Humane Society. Discussion included the cost of non-dog stray contracts, with one committee member noting that eight mice cost $175 each to process through the Humane Society. A suggestion was made to require animals be brought in by law enforcement to be billed to the municipality.

Staff member Renee reported the farmers market has been going well. She has been present the last three Saturdays from 6 a.m. for 6-7 hours. The market is full with food trucks, coffee vendors, craft people, and some early produce. Concerns were raised about parking on Alderson and Newport streets. Staff clarified parking is only allowed on the west side of Alderson, though the east side is still signed no parking.

Evan announced he is stepping down from the committee as he and his wife are moving away from Weston, though staying in the metro area. He thanked the committee for the opportunity to serve. The chair noted there are now multiple open spots on this and other committees and encouraged members to recruit interested citizens.

actionItems: CDA to pass resolution this summer to call TIFF bonds due October 1st
Clerk's office to continue recruiting one more part-time employee
Clerk planning to propose chief inspector wage increase from $13 to $15/hour for next election term
Recruit citizens to fill open committee spots
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, June 15th at 4:30 p.m.

title: Community Life and Public Safety
overview: The Village of Weston Community Life and Public Safety Committee approved amendments to the nuisance ordinance, recommended an e-bike ordinance with emoto restrictions, and authorized razing a condemned structure at 4104 Shore Avenue. The committee also discussed overnight parking regulations and a crosswalk request, deferring both items for further research.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Roll call
Public comments
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
Acknowledgment of department reports (Code Enforcement, Fire/EMS, Police)
Proposed amendments to Chapter 50 Nuisances
Proposed e-bicycle ordinance in Chapter 82
Section 82.107 overnight parking regulations discussion
Crosswalk request on Alderson Street
Mobile food vendors fire inspection amendment
Address corrections for E911 compliance
Raze request for 4104 Shore Avenue

discussions.body: Jim Senalt spoke regarding several agenda items. He criticized the new farmers market location, stating it would destroy grassy areas and that parking vehicles on grass may violate village code. He questioned ADA compliance for handicap parking on grass rather than asphalt. He also asked about the origin of fire inspections for food trucks and raised concerns about code enforcement cases being old without visible progress.

Code Enforcement reported that 3020 Mountain View Avenue will be removed from the enforcement roll after the property sale completed and new owner submitted repair permits. Police Chief Hunt reported 2,394 calls in March (77 per day), 410 traffic stops, and 281 citations. The department has hired its 40th officer, with the 38th starting last week, 39th in May, and 40th around January. Two SROs work on zoning violations during summer for all three municipalities. Motion to acknowledge reports 5, 6, and 7 passed unanimously.

The committee reviewed final amendments including tree limb height requirements of 14 feet over streets and 10 feet over walkways. Motion by Olsen to recommend approval to the Board of Trustees, seconded by Campkey. Motion passed unanimously.

The committee discussed the proposed e-bike ordinance for Chapter 82. Chief Hunt expressed concern about emotos (electric dirt bike-style vehicles), stating they should not be allowed on roadways or sidewalks as they are essentially unlicensed dirt bikes. Discussion included whether electric scooters would be affected. The committee agreed to prohibit emotos from roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property. Motion by Olsen to recommend approval with emoto restrictions, seconded by Sherwanka. Motion passed unanimously.

Trustee Tolson presented research showing Weston's 12-hour overnight parking window (4 AM to 4 PM) is significantly longer than surrounding municipalities, which typically have 4-5 hour windows. Public Works Director Michael noted plowing rarely occurs before 4-5 AM and suggested signing specific problem areas instead of village-wide restrictions. Chief Hunt preferred keeping overnight restrictions to give officers time to clear vehicles before plowing. The committee deferred the item to July for Michael to identify specific problem areas requiring signage.

Mount Olive Church requested a crosswalk between the church and DC Everest high school administrative parking lot. Staff estimated $2,000-$3,000 for a basic crosswalk, or $25,000-$30,000 for a lighted system. Chief Hunt reported no known incidents at the location. The committee agreed to approve a crosswalk only if Mount Olive covers the installation cost. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Sherwanka. Motion passed unanimously.

Fire Chief explained a multi-municipality fire inspection program with Stevens Point, Marshfield, Wisconsin Rapids, Clover, and Rib Mountain. One inspection with a sticker would be valid across all participating municipalities. Fire Marshal Marty already inspected one truck that failed due to galvanized steel LP piping and improperly secured propane containers. Motion by Sherwanka to approve Ordinance 26-8, seconded by Campkey. Motion passed unanimously.

Staff presented three address corrections for E911 compliance: 5915 Delicoski Street (duplex needing separate addresses), Ross Avenue properties out of sequence, and 2211 Radkey Avenue for a new duplex. Chief Hunt noted an additional addressing issue at Weston Villa Apartments on Delicoski with eight separate addresses for one structure. Motion passed unanimously to recommend corrections to the Board.

Staff requested authorization to begin raze proceedings on a structure abandoned since 2010-2011. The fire department previously declined a training burn due to unsafe conditions. Staff noted chickens had been housed in the structure. Current owners are up to date on taxes and prefer the village proceed with demolition, with costs to be placed on the tax roll. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Campkey. Motion passed unanimously.

publicComment: Jim Senalt spoke regarding concerns about the new farmers market location potentially destroying grass, ADA compliance issues with handicap parking on grass, questions about why fire inspections are being added for food trucks, and criticism that some code enforcement cases have been pending for years without visible progress.
actionItems: Chapter 50 Nuisance amendments recommended for Board of Trustees approval
E-bicycle ordinance with emoto restrictions recommended for Board approval
Michael to identify specific areas needing parking signage and return in July
Michael to contact Mount Olive Church about covering crosswalk installation costs
Ordinance 26-8 for mobile food vendor fire inspections approved
Address corrections for Delicoski Street, Ross Avenue, and Radkey Avenue recommended for Board approval
Staff authorized to begin raze proceedings for 4104 Shore Avenue
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, July 7th at 5:00 PM

The board was also expected to consider approval of the Riverview HVAC and roofing project and updates to the activities and athletics code.

Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present the Employee Handbook updates for the 2026-27 school year in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to review and consider approval of the updated handbook.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the preliminary budget for 2026-27 in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider the initial budget recommendation for the upcoming school year.
Karen Fochs was scheduled to present proposed changes to the 2026-2027 paid meal prices in an estimated 5 minutes. Supporting documentation included a meal price action sheet and PLE tool.

Dr. Jon Tomski and BJ Brandt were scheduled to present suggested changes to the Athletic and Activities Code in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the 2026-2027 Code of Conduct updates.

Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present proposed changes to Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of these policy revisions.

Ryan Urmanski, Director of Buildings and Grounds, was scheduled to present the Riverview HVAC and roofing project in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the RFP for this facility improvement project.

title: Special Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, this Special Meeting of the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address potential litigation in closed session under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). The board was expected to hold preliminary discussions regarding a legal matter before potentially reconvening in open session to take any necessary action.

agenda.item: Call to Order
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Preliminary Discussion Regarding Potential Litigation 19.85 (g)
Reconvene in Open Session, to take further action if necessary and appropriate
Adjourn

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to enter closed session to hold preliminary discussions regarding potential litigation, as permitted under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). This statute allows governmental bodies to convene in closed session when considering matters that could result in legal action against or on behalf of the district.
Following the closed session discussion, the board was expected to reconvene in open session to take any further action deemed necessary and appropriate based on the closed session deliberations.

publicComment: No public comment period was included on this agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to potentially take action following the closed session discussion regarding potential litigation

title: Regular Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address several significant action items at their May 11, 2026 regular meeting, including approval of the 2026-27 budget reconciliation plan, teacher contract renewals, and wage increases for non-teaching staff. The board was also expected to consider renewal of the WAMCS charter school contract for 2026-2031 and enter closed session regarding personnel matters including non-renewal notices and contract evaluations.

agenda.item: Call to Order
Roll Call
Pledge of Allegiance: Jim Bouche, President
Reading of the Mission Statement
Excellence in Action: Maine Elementary
Public and Student Comment
Approve Consent Agenda (Action Requested)

New Business

Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Adjourn

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Maine Elementary as part of the district's Excellence in Action program, which typically highlights achievements and initiatives at individual schools.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was expected to present a summary report of all legal counsel expenses incurred during the third quarter of 2025-2026. The report was described as broken down by law firm and by type of legal advice sought, requiring no action as a written report with an estimated 2-minute presentation time.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the budget reconciliation plan for budgeting purposes, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 3 minutes with action requested from the board.
Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present a list of teacher contracts for approval for the 2026-27 school year. The presentation was estimated at 5 minutes with action requested from the board.

Tabatha Gundrum was expected to present a recommendation for a 2.63% wage adjustment for all employee groups in the District except teachers, in accordance with the budget reconciliation plan. The agenda noted that teacher negotiations would commence soon with a separate action at a later date, with this presentation estimated at 5 minutes.

Elizabeth Channel, WAMCS Head of School, was scheduled to present the 2026-2031 contract renewal for the Wausau Area Montessori Charter School, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 2 minutes with action requested.

The board was scheduled to enter closed session pursuant to Wisconsin Statutes sections 19.85(1)(c), (e), and (g) to discuss final notice of non-renewal and contract evaluation matters, with plans to reconvene in open session to take further action if necessary.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.

title: City Council Meeting
overview: The Wausau Common Council approved mayoral appointments to multiple boards and commissions, passed a consent agenda including bike rack request procedures and special assessments for street construction, but saw contentious debate over proposed amendments to council standing rules with multiple failed motions. The council voted 11-0 on consent items while rule suspension votes were divided, with one failing 5-6.

agenda.item: Arbor Day Proclamation
Week of the Young Child Proclamation
National Preservation Month Proclamation
Workers Memorial Day Proclamation
March 24, 2026 Regular Common Council Minutes
April 14, 2026 Regular Common Council Minutes
Joint Resolution from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Approving the Bike Rack Request Form
Resolution from the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects
Ordinance from the Common Council to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council

Confirming Appointments of the Mayor of the City of Wausau to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board and Ethics Board

discussions.body: The council approved minutes from both the March 24, 2026 and April 14, 2026 regular meetings. The motion passed unanimously 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Tom Neal.

The consent agenda was approved 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Bruce Trueblood. This included the Joint Resolution Approving the Bike Rack Request Form from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and Infrastructure & Facilities Committee, as well as the Resolution Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects.

The council voted to suspend Rule 11(A) regarding referral of ordinances, passing 8-3 with Matt Hoenecke, Lou Larson, and Bruce Trueblood voting no. However, the motion to suspend Rule 21 regarding amending of the rules failed 5-6, with Carol Lukens, Michael Martens, Andrew Wiskowski, Kristin Slonski, Sarah Watson, and Lou Larson voting against.

discussions.item: Ordinance to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council
discussions.body: This item saw extensive procedural debate with multiple votes. A motion to approve as amended failed, as did a motion to refer back to the Rules Review Committee. An amendment by Alder Watson subject to attorney review passed, as did a subsequent amendment by Alder Tierney. An amendment proposed by Alder Neal failed.

The council approved the Mayor's appointments to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board, and Ethics Board. The motion passed.

publicComment: Public comment was included on the agenda both before the business meeting (preregistered citizens) and after the business meeting.
